name: NEOFACTO Senior React / SharePoint Interview
description: Technical interview preparation based only on the advertised Senior React Developer requirements: React, modern JavaScript, Jest and React Testing Library, SharePoint Online and SPFx, Microsoft 365 integration, accessibility, shared component libraries, performance, enterprise delivery, documentation, and stakeholder communication.

topics.name: React Component Architecture
topics.description: Designing reusable, maintainable React components and scalable frontend boundaries.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React component architecture is the way a frontend application is divided into components, features, and layers. A component should have a clear responsibility and a predictable public API through its props. Small presentational components can focus on rendering, while feature components coordinate data, state, and business behavior. Good architecture is not about making every component as small as possible. It is about creating boundaries that make the code easy to understand, test, reuse, and change.

## What problem does it solve?

A React application often starts with a few simple screens and then grows across many developers and teams. Without clear boundaries, components become tightly coupled, state is duplicated, and a change in one area unexpectedly breaks another. A sound component architecture reduces duplication, keeps business rules in sensible places, and allows shared components to evolve without forcing every consumer to know their internal details.

## How it works

Begin by separating reusable UI from feature-specific behavior. Pass data and callbacks through explicit props, and prefer composition over large components with many configuration flags. Keep state close to where it is used. Lift it to a common parent when several children need the same value, use context for stable values needed across a subtree, and use a global store only for genuinely application-wide or cross-feature state. Organize a larger codebase by business feature so that each feature owns its components, hooks, tests, and data access. Shared folders should contain only code that is truly reused. Stable interfaces between features make independent development easier.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include creating one large component that handles rendering, networking, validation, and business logic; moving all state into a global store; putting feature-specific code into a generic shared folder; and building overly configurable components with many boolean props. Another mistake is adding abstractions before there are real repeated patterns. Reuse should follow demonstrated needs, not guesses about the future.

## Key terms

Component boundary: the public interface and responsibility of a component. Composition: building complex interfaces by combining smaller components. Local state: state owned near the component that uses it. Lifted state: shared state moved to a common parent. Context: a way to provide values through a component subtree. Global state: application-wide state managed outside individual components. Feature-based structure: organizing code around business capabilities rather than file types.

topics.name: Modern JavaScript and React Hooks
topics.description: JavaScript execution, asynchronous behavior, state, effects, and hooks correctness.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Modern JavaScript and React Hooks provide the language features and React APIs used to build interactive frontend applications. JavaScript runs synchronous code on a single call stack, while the browser and runtime handle timers, network operations, and other asynchronous work. Promises and async functions make that work easier to express. React Hooks such as useState, useEffect, useRef, and custom hooks let function components manage state, side effects, and reusable behavior.

## What problem does it solve?

Frontend applications must respond to user actions, load remote data, update the screen, and clean up resources without blocking the browser. Hooks give React components a consistent way to manage these responsibilities. Understanding JavaScript execution is important because incorrect assumptions about timing can cause stale data, race conditions, missing error handling, or an interface that updates in the wrong order.

## How it works

Synchronous JavaScript runs first. Promise callbacks are placed in the microtask queue and normally run after the current call stack is empty but before timer callbacks from the task queue. In React, useState schedules state updates and triggers rendering. useEffect runs after React commits a render and should be used to synchronize the component with something outside React, such as a request, subscription, timer, or browser API. Its dependency list describes the reactive values used by the effect. An effect may return a cleanup function to cancel or undo previous work. For data loading, track loading and error states, handle cancellation or stale responses, and avoid updating the interface with the result of an outdated request.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include using useEffect to calculate values that could be derived during rendering, leaving dependencies out to silence warnings, creating an infinite loop by updating a dependency inside the effect, and forgetting to remove listeners or cancel requests. Other mistakes include assuming state updates are immediately available, ignoring rejected promises, and allowing an older network response to overwrite newer data.

## Key terms

Call stack: where synchronous functions execute. Event loop: the mechanism that schedules asynchronous callbacks. Microtask: a high-priority callback, commonly created by a Promise. Task queue: the queue used by timers and many browser events. Side effect: work that interacts with something outside rendering. Dependency array: values that determine when an effect runs again. Cleanup function: code that releases resources from an earlier effect. Race condition: a bug caused by operations completing in an unexpected order.

topics.name: Frontend Testing
topics.description: Unit and integration testing with Jest and React Testing Library.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Frontend testing checks that user interfaces behave correctly as the code changes. Jest is a JavaScript test runner and assertion library. React Testing Library renders React components and provides queries and user interactions that encourage tests to use the interface in a way similar to a real user. A frontend test strategy normally combines unit tests, integration tests, and a smaller number of end-to-end tests.

## What problem does it solve?

Frontend code contains many states: loading, success, empty results, validation errors, failed requests, permissions, and user interactions. Manual testing alone is slow and inconsistent. Automated tests catch regressions, give developers confidence to refactor, and describe the expected behavior of important components and workflows.

## How it works

A good React Testing Library test renders a component, finds elements through accessible roles, labels, or visible text, performs realistic interactions, and checks the result visible to the user. External boundaries such as network requests can be controlled with test doubles or a request-mocking tool. Asynchronous assertions wait for the interface to reach the expected state instead of using arbitrary delays. Unit tests are useful for small pure functions or isolated rules. Integration tests cover several components and their interactions. End-to-end tests run the application in a browser and verify a small number of critical user journeys across the whole system.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include testing private implementation details, selecting elements through CSS classes, asserting internal state, mocking every child component, and writing snapshots that are too large to review. Another mistake is using fixed timeouts for asynchronous behavior instead of waiting for a meaningful UI change. Tests can also become misleading when they reproduce the implementation rather than verifying the required behavior.

## Key terms

Test runner: software that discovers and executes tests. Assertion: a statement about an expected result. Accessible query: finding an element by role, label, or user-visible text. Mock: a controlled replacement for an external dependency. Unit test: a test of a small isolated behavior. Integration test: a test of connected parts working together. End-to-end test: a browser-level test of a complete workflow. Regression: previously working behavior that breaks after a change.

topics.name: Frontend Performance and Maintainability
topics.description: Diagnosing and improving rendering, loading, and long-term code quality.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Frontend performance is the speed and responsiveness experienced by a user, from the first page load to later interactions. In React applications it includes download size, parsing and execution time, network requests, rendering work, and the cost of updating components. Maintainability is closely related because clear code and measured optimizations are easier to preserve than a collection of unexplained performance tricks.

## What problem does it solve?

A slow application delays useful content, feels unresponsive, consumes more device resources, and may perform especially badly on slower networks or hardware. Guessing at fixes can waste time or make the code more complex without helping users. A performance process identifies the actual bottleneck and applies the smallest change that produces a measurable improvement.

## How it works

Start with evidence from production monitoring, browser performance tools, network traces, and React Profiler. Determine whether the delay comes from downloading assets, waiting for an API, executing JavaScript, rendering a large tree, or repeatedly updating components. Reduce initial load with route or feature-level code splitting, lazy loading, efficient assets, and removal of unused dependencies. Reduce unnecessary rendering by keeping state close to its consumers, using stable component boundaries, and avoiding updates that affect large parts of the tree. Memoization can help when an expensive calculation or stable child is proven to be a bottleneck, but it also has a cost and should be measured.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include optimizing before measuring, wrapping every component in memo, using useMemo and useCallback automatically, and focusing only on bundle size while ignoring slow APIs or expensive rendering. Other mistakes include loading every feature on the first page, rendering very large lists without virtualization, and using unstable keys that cause React to recreate items.

## Key terms

Profiling: measuring where time and resources are spent. React Profiler: a tool for examining React render work. Code splitting: dividing JavaScript into chunks loaded when needed. Lazy loading: delaying a resource until it is required. Rerender: React running a component again to calculate its output. Memoization: reusing a previous result when inputs have not changed. Virtualization: rendering only the visible part of a large list. Performance budget: an agreed limit for metrics such as bundle size or loading time.

topics.name: Frontend Accessibility
topics.description: Building inclusive, keyboard-accessible, standards-aware enterprise interfaces.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Frontend accessibility means designing and implementing interfaces that people with different abilities can perceive, understand, navigate, and operate. It includes support for keyboard users, screen readers, zoom, high contrast, and users who need clear language or predictable interactions. Semantic HTML provides much of this behavior automatically, while ARIA can describe custom widgets when native elements are not enough.

## What problem does it solve?

An interface that works only with a mouse, relies only on color, or has missing labels can block people from completing essential tasks. Accessibility also improves general usability and helps enterprise applications meet organizational and legal requirements. Building it into components from the beginning is more reliable and less expensive than repairing every screen later.

## How it works

Prefer native elements such as button, input, select, and dialog-compatible patterns because browsers already provide keyboard and assistive-technology behavior. Ensure every form control has an accessible name, focus is visible, and the tab order follows the visual and logical order. Custom widgets need the expected keyboard controls, roles, states, and focus management. A modal should move focus inside when opened, keep keyboard focus within it while active, and return focus to the element that opened it. Validation errors should be clear, associated with the relevant fields, and announced when appropriate. Automated checks can catch common problems, but manual keyboard testing and screen-reader checks are still necessary.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include using a div as a button, removing the focus outline, adding ARIA where native HTML would be simpler, relying only on color to communicate status, and using placeholders instead of labels. Other mistakes include trapping focus incorrectly, failing to restore focus after a modal closes, and showing validation errors that are not connected to their fields.

## Key terms

Semantic HTML: elements whose meaning and behavior are understood by browsers and assistive technology. Accessible name: the text used to identify a control. Keyboard navigation: operating an interface without a mouse. Focus management: controlling where keyboard focus moves. ARIA: attributes that describe roles, states, and relationships for accessibility. Screen reader: software that presents interface content through speech or braille. WCAG: widely used guidelines for web accessibility. Focus trap: keeping focus within an active modal or similar contained interaction.

topics.name: SharePoint Online and SPFx
topics.description: Building and delivering React web parts in SharePoint Online with SPFx.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

SharePoint Online is Microsoft 365's platform for company sites, intranets, document libraries, lists, and collaboration. The SharePoint Framework, usually called SPFx, is the supported development model for adding custom client-side solutions to SharePoint. An SPFx web part is a configurable block that can be placed on a SharePoint page. React is commonly used to build the user interface inside that web part.

## What problem does it solve?

Organizations often need interfaces that go beyond the standard SharePoint features, such as a custom dashboard, request form, document view, or employee tool. SPFx lets developers build these features while still using the SharePoint page, user identity, permissions, configuration, and deployment model. It provides a controlled way to integrate custom frontend code into Microsoft 365 rather than maintaining a separate unrelated website for every internal need.

## How it works

An SPFx project contains a web-part class and the frontend code it renders. The web-part class receives the SharePoint context, configuration properties, and lifecycle events. It can create a React root and pass only the data or services required by the React application. This keeps most components independent of SPFx and easier to test. The property pane allows page editors to configure values such as a title, list, or filter. The solution is built into a SharePoint package, deployed through an app catalog, approved as required, and then made available on SharePoint sites. Cleanup should unmount the React tree and release subscriptions, listeners, or other resources when the web part is removed.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include importing SPFx context into every React component, placing business logic directly in the web-part class, exposing secrets in client-side code, and assuming the current user can access every data source. Other mistakes include ignoring the web-part lifecycle, failing to clean up resources, hard-coding site URLs, and testing only in a local environment without checking the real tenant and permission setup.

## Key terms

SharePoint Online: the cloud version of SharePoint in Microsoft 365. SPFx: SharePoint Framework. Web part: a configurable component placed on a SharePoint page. Web-part context: information and services supplied by the SharePoint host. Property pane: the editor used to configure a web part. App catalog: the location from which SPFx packages are deployed and managed. Tenant: an organization's Microsoft 365 environment. Service layer: code that hides platform-specific data access from UI components.

topics.name: Microsoft 365 Integration and Security
topics.description: Integrating frontend solutions with SharePoint, Microsoft Graph, permissions, and internal APIs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Microsoft 365 integration connects a frontend solution to services such as SharePoint sites, lists, document libraries, Teams-related resources, and Microsoft Graph. Microsoft Graph is a common API for accessing many Microsoft 365 services. An SPFx application can also call existing internal APIs when business data or rules live outside Microsoft 365.

## What problem does it solve?

Enterprise information is usually spread across several systems. A useful frontend may need documents from SharePoint, profile information from Microsoft Graph, and business records from an internal service. Integration brings that information into one workflow while respecting identity, permissions, security rules, and service limits. Choosing the correct source prevents duplicated data and avoids building a new API for information that already has a supported owner.

## How it works

Choose a SharePoint list for structured site-owned data, a document library for files and document metadata, Microsoft Graph for supported Microsoft 365 resources, and an internal API for domain logic or data owned by another system. The browser uses the signed-in user's identity or an approved application permission flow. Request only the permissions required for the feature and rely on the server or platform to enforce authorization; hiding a button in the UI is not security. Integrations should handle unavailable services, partial results, throttling, and changing response shapes. Use retries only for temporary failures, apply backoff, respect retry guidance from the service, validate responses, and present a useful error or partial state to the user.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include requesting broad permissions for convenience, treating authentication as proof that the user may access every record, storing secrets in frontend code, and duplicating authoritative data in several systems. Other mistakes include retrying every error immediately, sending too many requests in parallel, ignoring pagination, depending directly on an unstable response shape, and showing a blank screen when only one data source fails.

## Key terms

Microsoft Graph: a unified API for Microsoft 365 services. SharePoint list: structured records stored within a SharePoint site. Document library: SharePoint storage for files and metadata. Authentication: confirming who the caller is. Authorization: deciding what the caller may do. Delegated permission: access performed on behalf of the signed-in user. Application permission: access performed by an application without a user. Least privilege: granting only the access needed. Throttling: a service temporarily limiting request volume. Exponential backoff: increasing the delay between retries.

topics.name: Design Systems and Shared Component Libraries
topics.description: Consuming and extending shared UI libraries while preserving consistency and usability.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A design system is a shared collection of design decisions, guidelines, tokens, and reusable UI components. A component library is the coded part of that system, such as buttons, form controls, dialogs, tables, and layout primitives. It gives product teams a common visual language and a tested foundation for building consistent interfaces.

## What problem does it solve?

Without a shared system, teams repeatedly build slightly different versions of the same controls. This creates inconsistent behavior, duplicated work, accessibility problems, and expensive maintenance. A design system allows improvements and fixes to be made once and distributed to many products, while still giving teams a clear process for handling requirements that the existing library does not cover.

## How it works

First check whether an existing component already supports the required behavior. Reuse it when possible. Extend it through documented composition or supported variants when the need is broadly useful and fits the component's purpose. Build a feature-specific component outside the shared library when the behavior belongs to only one domain. A stable component API uses clear names, sensible defaults, controlled extension points, and predictable accessibility behavior. Changes should be documented, tested, and released in a way that consumers can adopt safely. When implementing a Figma design, identify all states, including loading, empty, error, disabled, hover, focus, and responsive behavior. Discuss missing states with design and product rather than inventing important business behavior silently.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include copying a shared component and modifying it locally, adding a new variant for one narrow screen, exposing too many styling details, and creating a component with many unrelated boolean options. Other mistakes include treating Figma as a complete behavioral specification, ignoring keyboard and error states, and introducing breaking changes without migration guidance.

## Key terms

Design token: a named value for a design decision such as spacing, typography, or color. Component library: reusable coded UI components. Variant: a supported form of a component. Composition: combining components instead of adding every behavior to one component. Public API: the props and behaviors that consumers depend on. Breaking change: a change that requires consumers to update their code. Storybook: a tool for developing and documenting components in isolation. Design handoff: the process of turning design specifications into production behavior.

topics.name: Enterprise Frontend Delivery
topics.description: Testing, validation, troubleshooting, documentation, and collaboration across environments and teams.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Enterprise frontend delivery is the complete process of moving a frontend change from development into a reliable production service. It includes collaboration, coding standards, testing, security review, environment configuration, deployment, troubleshooting, documentation, and handover. The work often involves developers, technical leads, business analysts, QA, operations teams, and external service providers.

## What problem does it solve?

A feature that works on one developer's machine may fail in test or production because environments have different configuration, permissions, data, network rules, or deployment versions. Enterprise delivery creates repeatable checks and shared information so that changes can be validated, supported, and transferred between teams. It also makes technical decisions understandable to people with different responsibilities.

## How it works

A change moves through defined environments such as development, test, acceptance, and production. Configuration that differs by environment should be explicit and should not be hard-coded into the application. When a failure appears only in one environment, compare the deployed version, runtime configuration, network requests, permissions, browser errors, and backend logs before changing code. Handover documentation should explain the solution's purpose, architecture, dependencies, configuration, deployment steps, permissions, monitoring, known limitations, and troubleshooting process. When discussing a trade-off, keep the underlying facts consistent but adapt the explanation: a technical lead may need architectural detail, a business analyst may need effects on requirements, and a non-technical stakeholder may need impact on risk, cost, and delivery time.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include assuming every environment is identical, changing code before confirming what version is deployed, storing environment values in source files, and treating acceptance testing as only QA's responsibility. Other mistakes include documentation that describes only how the code works but not how to operate it, and presenting a technical preference without explaining business impact or alternatives.

## Key terms

Environment: a separate deployment stage with its own configuration and data. Acceptance environment: the stage where a solution is validated against business expectations. Configuration drift: unintended differences between environments. Handover: transferring enough knowledge for another team to operate and maintain a solution. Runbook: practical instructions for deployment or troubleshooting. Observability: logs, metrics, and traces used to understand system behavior. Technical trade-off: a choice that improves one quality while accepting a cost elsewhere. Knowledge transfer: structured sharing of implementation and operational knowledge.

questions.title: Designing a reusable React component
questions.prompt: How would you design a reusable React component that will be used across several enterprise pages?
questions.answerStructureHint: Responsibilities, API design, composition, quality controls.

expectedPoints.label: Defines a focused component API
expectedPoints.description: Keeps responsibilities clear and exposes stable, typed props.

expectedPoints.label: Uses composition appropriately
expectedPoints.description: Prefers composition and slots over large sets of conditional props.

expectedPoints.label: Accounts for quality constraints
expectedPoints.description: Includes accessibility, tests, documentation, and consistent styling.

followUps.prompt: When would you split this component into smaller components?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first define one clear responsibility and a small typed prop API. I would use composition for variation instead of adding many boolean props, keep domain logic outside the visual component, and follow the shared design tokens. I would also provide accessible defaults, tests for public behavior, and documentation with examples so other teams can use it safely.

questions.title: Choosing state boundaries
questions.prompt: How do you decide whether state should be local, lifted to a parent, placed in context, or stored in a global state solution?
questions.answerStructureHint: Scope, type of state, trade-offs.

expectedPoints.label: Matches state location to scope
expectedPoints.description: Places state at the narrowest level that needs to own it.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes state types
expectedPoints.description: Separates local UI state, server state, and cross-cutting application state.

expectedPoints.label: Explains trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: Considers coupling, rerenders, testability, and debugging.

followUps.prompt: What problems can appear when too much state is put into React Context?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I keep state as local as possible. I lift it only when siblings need the same source of truth, use context for stable cross-cutting concerns with limited update frequency, and use a dedicated store for complex application-wide workflows. I treat server state separately because caching, invalidation, and refetching have different needs from client UI state.

questions.title: Structuring a large frontend
questions.prompt: How would you structure a growing React codebase so that several teams can work on it without creating tight coupling?
questions.answerStructureHint: Feature boundaries, dependency rules, shared libraries.

expectedPoints.label: Creates feature boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Organizes business capabilities into cohesive feature modules.

expectedPoints.label: Controls dependencies
expectedPoints.description: Defines allowed dependency directions and avoids circular imports.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps shared code intentional
expectedPoints.description: Moves only genuinely reusable components and utilities into shared libraries.

followUps.prompt: How would you prevent a shared folder from becoming a dumping ground?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would organize the application around business features with clear ownership and public entry points. Shared UI primitives and infrastructure would live in dedicated libraries, but feature-specific logic would stay inside the feature. I would enforce dependency rules through linting and reviews, avoid circular imports, and keep contracts between modules explicit.

questions.title: Using useEffect correctly
questions.prompt: What is useEffect for, and what common mistakes do you watch for when using it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Purpose, dependencies, cleanup.

expectedPoints.label: Defines the purpose of effects
expectedPoints.description: Uses effects to synchronize React with external systems.

expectedPoints.label: Handles dependencies correctly
expectedPoints.description: Includes real dependencies and avoids stale closures.

expectedPoints.label: Cleans up resources
expectedPoints.description: Cancels subscriptions, timers, or obsolete requests when needed.

followUps.prompt: When would you remove an effect and calculate a value during render instead?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I use useEffect to synchronize a component with something outside React, such as a subscription, timer, browser API, or request lifecycle. I avoid using it for values that can be derived during render. I include all real dependencies, watch for stale closures, and return cleanup code for subscriptions, timers, or obsolete requests.

questions.title: JavaScript event loop and promises
questions.prompt: Can you explain how the JavaScript event loop handles synchronous code, promises, and timers?
questions.answerStructureHint: Call stack, microtasks versus tasks, UI impact.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the call stack
expectedPoints.description: States that synchronous work runs on the call stack first.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes task queues
expectedPoints.description: Explains that promise callbacks run as microtasks before timer tasks.

expectedPoints.label: Connects behavior to frontend issues
expectedPoints.description: Relates long synchronous work to blocked rendering and input.

followUps.prompt: Why can a resolved Promise callback run before a setTimeout callback with zero delay?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: Synchronous code runs on the call stack until it is empty. Promise callbacks are queued as microtasks and are processed before the next task such as a setTimeout callback. Rendering also needs the main thread, so a long synchronous task can delay painting and user input even when asynchronous APIs are used elsewhere.

questions.title: Reliable asynchronous UI flows
questions.prompt: How would you implement an asynchronous data-loading flow in React so that it handles loading, errors, retries, and stale responses correctly?
questions.answerStructureHint: UI states, race prevention, retry policy.

expectedPoints.label: Models explicit request states
expectedPoints.description: Represents loading, success, empty, and error states clearly.

expectedPoints.label: Prevents stale updates
expectedPoints.description: Uses cancellation, request identity, or a server-state library to avoid races.

expectedPoints.label: Uses safe retry behavior
expectedPoints.description: Retries selectively with backoff and avoids duplicate side effects.

followUps.prompt: How would you prevent a response from an old search query from replacing a newer result?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would model loading, success, empty, and error states explicitly. I would cancel obsolete requests with AbortController or use a server-state library that tracks request identity, so an older response cannot overwrite newer data. Retries should be limited and mainly used for transient failures, with backoff and only when the operation is safe to repeat.

questions.title: React Testing Library philosophy
questions.prompt: What should a good React Testing Library test focus on, and what should it avoid?
questions.answerStructureHint: User behavior, query strategy, implementation details.

expectedPoints.label: Tests observable behavior
expectedPoints.description: Tests what a user can see and do rather than implementation details.

expectedPoints.label: Uses semantic queries
expectedPoints.description: Prefers role, label, and visible text queries.

expectedPoints.label: Avoids brittle implementation tests
expectedPoints.description: Does not assert internal state, private methods, or component structure.

followUps.prompt: Why is getByRole often preferable to a test id?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: A good React Testing Library test interacts with the component as a user would and asserts visible behavior. I prefer semantic queries such as role, label, and accessible name. I avoid testing internal state, private functions, or exact component structure because those tests break during harmless refactoring.

questions.title: Testing an asynchronous component
questions.prompt: How would you test a component that loads data, shows a spinner, and then displays either results or an error?
questions.answerStructureHint: Mock boundary, asynchronous assertions, outcome coverage.

expectedPoints.label: Controls the API boundary
expectedPoints.description: Mocks the network boundary or uses a request-level test server.

expectedPoints.label: Waits for UI changes correctly
expectedPoints.description: Uses findBy or waitFor rather than arbitrary delays.

expectedPoints.label: Covers meaningful outcomes
expectedPoints.description: Tests success, empty, error, and optionally retry behavior.

followUps.prompt: Why would you prefer a network-level mock over mocking internal hooks?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would control the API at the network boundary, preferably with a request-level mock such as MSW. The test would assert the initial loading state, then use findBy or waitFor to verify the result. I would add separate cases for successful data, an empty result, an error response, and retry behavior if that is part of the UI.

questions.title: Choosing unit, integration, and end-to-end tests
questions.prompt: How do you decide whether a frontend behavior should be covered by a unit test, an integration test, or an end-to-end test?
questions.answerStructureHint: Scope, confidence versus cost, risk.

expectedPoints.label: Matches test type to scope
expectedPoints.description: Uses unit tests for isolated logic, integration tests for component collaboration, and E2E for critical journeys.

expectedPoints.label: Balances confidence and cost
expectedPoints.description: Considers execution speed, maintenance, flakiness, and realism.

expectedPoints.label: Prioritizes by risk
expectedPoints.description: Gives stronger coverage to high-impact workflows and complex logic.

followUps.prompt: Which frontend behaviors would you avoid covering only with end-to-end tests?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I use unit tests for pure or isolated logic, integration tests for components working with their real collaborators, and end-to-end tests for a small number of critical user journeys. The decision is based on the confidence needed versus execution time, maintenance cost, and flakiness. High-impact or failure-prone workflows receive the strongest coverage.

questions.title: Diagnosing a slow React page
questions.prompt: A React page feels slow in production. How would you investigate the cause before changing the code?
questions.answerStructureHint: Evidence, bottleneck classification, validation.

expectedPoints.label: Measures before optimizing
expectedPoints.description: Reproduces the issue and collects browser and production evidence.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies the bottleneck
expectedPoints.description: Separates network, JavaScript, rendering, layout, and backend latency.

expectedPoints.label: Validates the improvement
expectedPoints.description: Compares before and after metrics and checks regressions.

followUps.prompt: How would you distinguish an API latency problem from a rendering problem?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first reproduce the issue under realistic conditions and gather evidence from browser performance tools, network traces, React Profiler, and production monitoring. I would determine whether the delay comes from network or backend latency, JavaScript long tasks, excessive React rendering, or layout and painting. After a targeted change, I would compare the same metrics and add a guard such as a performance budget where practical.

questions.title: Reducing unnecessary rerenders
questions.prompt: What causes unnecessary rerenders in React, and how would you reduce them without overusing memoization?
questions.answerStructureHint: Causes, architectural fixes, selective memoization.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies common causes
expectedPoints.description: Mentions parent rerenders, changed object identities, context updates, and broad subscriptions.

expectedPoints.label: Improves component boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Localizes state and narrows subscriptions before adding memoization.

expectedPoints.label: Uses memoization selectively
expectedPoints.description: Applies memo, useMemo, or useCallback only after measuring a meaningful cost.

followUps.prompt: When can useCallback make code more complex without improving performance?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: Rerenders can come from parent updates, new object or callback identities, context changes, or broad store subscriptions. I first improve boundaries by moving state closer to where it is used, splitting expensive subtrees, and selecting only needed data. I use memoization only when profiling shows a real cost because memoization also adds complexity and comparison work.

questions.title: Improving initial load performance
questions.prompt: How would you improve the initial load time of a large enterprise React application?
questions.answerStructureHint: Measure bundle, split code, optimize dependencies and assets.

expectedPoints.label: Analyzes the loading path
expectedPoints.description: Uses bundle analysis and loading metrics to identify the largest contributors.

expectedPoints.label: Reduces upfront JavaScript
expectedPoints.description: Uses route or feature-level code splitting and lazy loading.

expectedPoints.label: Optimizes dependencies and assets
expectedPoints.description: Removes unused code, controls dependencies, and optimizes static assets and caching.

followUps.prompt: How do you choose a useful code-splitting boundary?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would start with a bundle report and loading waterfall to find the biggest costs. I would split code at route or feature boundaries, lazy-load noncritical functionality, and remove or replace oversized dependencies. I would also optimize images and fonts, use appropriate caching, and preload only truly critical resources, then compare real loading metrics after the change.

questions.title: Building an accessible interactive component
questions.prompt: What would you consider when implementing a custom interactive component such as a dropdown or modal?
questions.answerStructureHint: Semantics, keyboard behavior, focus management.

expectedPoints.label: Uses correct semantics
expectedPoints.description: Starts with native HTML and adds ARIA only where needed.

expectedPoints.label: Supports keyboard interaction
expectedPoints.description: Implements expected keyboard controls and visible focus.

expectedPoints.label: Manages focus correctly
expectedPoints.description: Moves, traps, and restores focus when the interaction requires it.

followUps.prompt: Why is adding an ARIA role to a div not enough to make it behave like a button?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would prefer a native element or an established accessible component before building a custom one. The component needs the correct role and accessible name, complete keyboard behavior, and a visible focus state. For a modal, I would move focus inside, keep it trapped while open, support Escape, and restore focus to the element that opened it.

questions.title: Accessible enterprise forms
questions.prompt: How would you make a complex form accessible, including validation errors?
questions.answerStructureHint: Labels, error communication, keyboard flow.

expectedPoints.label: Provides programmatic labels
expectedPoints.description: Associates every input with a clear label and instructions.

expectedPoints.label: Communicates errors accessibly
expectedPoints.description: Links errors to fields and provides a useful summary or announcement.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves usable keyboard flow
expectedPoints.description: Uses logical order, clear grouping, and avoids unexpected focus changes.

followUps.prompt: How would a screen-reader user discover all validation errors after submitting the form?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: Each control should have a visible programmatic label, and related fields should be grouped with fieldset and legend where appropriate. Validation errors should explain the problem, be associated with the field, and be announced or summarized after submission. The form should have a logical keyboard order and should not move focus unexpectedly.

questions.title: Validating accessibility
questions.prompt: How would you include accessibility checks in the development and testing process?
questions.answerStructureHint: Automated checks, manual validation, delivery process.

expectedPoints.label: Uses automated checks
expectedPoints.description: Adds linting and automated accessibility scans while recognizing their limits.

expectedPoints.label: Performs manual checks
expectedPoints.description: Tests keyboard navigation, focus, zoom, and screen-reader basics.

expectedPoints.label: Builds accessibility into delivery
expectedPoints.description: Uses accessible design-system components and acceptance criteria.

followUps.prompt: What important accessibility problems are automated tools unlikely to detect?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would add linting and axe-based checks to catch common problems, but I would not treat automation as complete coverage. I would manually test keyboard navigation, focus order, zoom, and important flows with a screen reader. Accessibility should also be part of acceptance criteria and shared components so it is addressed during implementation rather than only at the end.

questions.title: SPFx and React overview
questions.prompt: What is the SharePoint Framework, and how does a React component fit into an SPFx solution?
questions.answerStructureHint: Purpose, React integration, deployment flow.

expectedPoints.label: Defines SPFx
expectedPoints.description: Describes SPFx as the client-side extensibility model for SharePoint and Microsoft 365.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the React boundary
expectedPoints.description: Explains that the SPFx web part supplies lifecycle and context while React renders the UI.

expectedPoints.label: Explains deployment at a high level
expectedPoints.description: Mentions bundling, packaging, the app catalog, and adding the web part to a page.

followUps.prompt: What responsibilities should stay in the SPFx web part class, and what should stay in React components?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: SPFx is Microsoft’s client-side framework for extending SharePoint Online and related Microsoft 365 experiences. An SPFx web part provides the SharePoint lifecycle, configuration, and context, and it mounts a React component for the actual interface. The solution is built and packaged, deployed through the SharePoint app catalog, and then the web part can be added to an approved page.

questions.title: Using SPFx context and properties
questions.prompt: How would you pass SharePoint context and configurable web-part properties into a React application without tightly coupling every component to SPFx?
questions.answerStructureHint: Adapter boundary, typed props, testability.

expectedPoints.label: Creates a platform adapter
expectedPoints.description: Keeps SPFx-specific access in a small service or adapter layer.

expectedPoints.label: Passes typed application data
expectedPoints.description: Maps context and properties to minimal typed values required by React.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves testability
expectedPoints.description: Allows components to be tested without a live SharePoint environment.

followUps.prompt: What would be difficult to test if every component imported and used the SPFx context directly?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would keep the raw SPFx context inside a thin platform adapter or service. The web part would map configuration and context into small typed interfaces and pass those into the React application. Components would depend on application-level services rather than SPFx objects directly, which makes them easier to test and reuse outside a live SharePoint page.

questions.title: SPFx lifecycle and cleanup
questions.prompt: What lifecycle and cleanup concerns would you consider when mounting a React application inside an SPFx web part?
questions.answerStructureHint: React root lifecycle, external cleanup, property updates.

expectedPoints.label: Handles mounting and unmounting
expectedPoints.description: Renders on the correct host element and unmounts the React tree when disposed.

expectedPoints.label: Cleans external resources
expectedPoints.description: Removes listeners, subscriptions, timers, and pending requests.

expectedPoints.label: Responds to configuration changes
expectedPoints.description: Updates the UI correctly when web-part properties or context change.

followUps.prompt: What production symptoms can appear when a web part does not clean up subscriptions?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The web part should mount React into its own DOM element and unmount the React root when SharePoint disposes it. Effects and services must remove listeners, unsubscribe, clear timers, and abort obsolete requests. I would also make sure property-pane changes or relevant context changes produce a controlled rerender with updated typed props.

questions.title: Choosing a Microsoft 365 data source
questions.prompt: How would you decide whether to read data from a SharePoint list, a document library, Microsoft Graph, or an existing internal API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Data ownership, source capabilities, operational constraints.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies the system of record
expectedPoints.description: Chooses the source that owns the data and business rules.

expectedPoints.label: Matches the source to capabilities
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes list metadata, document storage, cross-M365 data, and domain APIs.

expectedPoints.label: Considers operational constraints
expectedPoints.description: Accounts for permissions, throttling, query limits, versioning, and supportability.

followUps.prompt: When would putting domain logic directly into a SharePoint list-based frontend become a maintenance problem?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would start with the authoritative owner of the data. SharePoint lists suit structured collaborative metadata, document libraries suit files and their metadata, Microsoft Graph is useful for cross-Microsoft 365 resources, and an internal API should remain the source when it owns domain rules. I would also compare permissions, throttling, query limits, versioning, and long-term support.

questions.title: Permissions and least privilege
questions.prompt: How would you approach authentication and permissions for an SPFx solution that reads Microsoft 365 or internal business data?
questions.answerStructureHint: User context, least privilege, no browser secrets.

expectedPoints.label: Uses the signed-in user context
expectedPoints.description: Understands delegated access and avoids handling user passwords or custom tokens unnecessarily.

expectedPoints.label: Applies least privilege
expectedPoints.description: Requests only the permissions needed and respects server-side authorization.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps secrets out of the browser
expectedPoints.description: Does not place confidential credentials or application secrets in frontend code.

followUps.prompt: Why is storing a secret in a frontend environment variable not secure?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would use the authenticated Microsoft 365 user context and delegated access where appropriate. The solution should request only the scopes it needs, while the data source still enforces authorization server-side. I would never embed a client secret or confidential credential in the frontend because browser code and configuration are visible to users.

questions.title: Resilient Microsoft 365 integration
questions.prompt: How would you make a frontend integration resilient to throttling, partial failures, and changing API responses?
questions.answerStructureHint: Failure states, safe retries, API contract boundary.

expectedPoints.label: Handles failures explicitly
expectedPoints.description: Maps authentication, permission, throttling, validation, and server failures to useful states.

expectedPoints.label: Retries safely
expectedPoints.description: Honors retry guidance, uses backoff, and retries only transient safe operations.

expectedPoints.label: Protects against contract changes
expectedPoints.description: Uses typed adapters, validation, and contract-focused tests.

followUps.prompt: Which HTTP operations would you be cautious about retrying automatically?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would map different failures to explicit UI states, especially permission problems and throttling. For transient failures, I would honor Retry-After where available, use bounded backoff, and retry only operations that are safe to repeat. A typed adapter with runtime validation and contract-focused tests would isolate the UI from response-shape changes.

questions.title: Using a shared component library
questions.prompt: When a shared component library already exists, how do you decide whether to reuse a component, extend it, or build a new one?
questions.answerStructureHint: Reuse fit, supported extension, governance.

expectedPoints.label: Evaluates reuse first
expectedPoints.description: Checks whether the existing component meets the user and accessibility requirements.

expectedPoints.label: Extends through supported APIs
expectedPoints.description: Uses composition, tokens, or documented extension points instead of forking.

expectedPoints.label: Coordinates new shared components
expectedPoints.description: Adds a new component only for a repeatable need and follows library governance.

followUps.prompt: What risks are introduced by copying a shared component into a feature and modifying it locally?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I first check whether an existing component can meet the user, design, and accessibility requirements. If variation is needed, I use supported props, composition, and design tokens rather than copying or forking it. I propose a new shared component only when the pattern is repeatable, with agreement from design and library maintainers.

questions.title: Designing a component-library API
questions.prompt: What makes the API of a shared UI component stable and easy for other teams to use?
questions.answerStructureHint: Typed API, accessible defaults, documentation and evolution.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps the API clear
expectedPoints.description: Uses descriptive typed props, safe defaults, and a limited set of supported variants.

expectedPoints.label: Provides accessible behavior by default
expectedPoints.description: Builds semantics, keyboard behavior, and focus handling into the component.

expectedPoints.label: Documents and versions behavior
expectedPoints.description: Provides examples, migration guidance, and a controlled deprecation process.

followUps.prompt: How would you remove a widely used prop without breaking consuming applications?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: A stable shared component has a small typed API with descriptive names, safe defaults, and only supported variants. Accessibility behavior should be built in rather than left to every consumer. The component needs examples, clear edge-case documentation, and a controlled deprecation and migration process when its API changes.

questions.title: Implementing a design specification
questions.prompt: How do you translate a UX or Figma specification into a maintainable production component when some states or edge cases are missing?
questions.answerStructureHint: Missing states, clarification, reusable implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies missing states
expectedPoints.description: Clarifies loading, empty, error, disabled, responsive, and interaction states.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies with stakeholders
expectedPoints.description: Raises focused questions and records agreed behavior with design or business.

expectedPoints.label: Maps design to reusable primitives
expectedPoints.description: Uses tokens and shared components rather than one-off styling.

followUps.prompt: What would you do if the visual design conflicts with an accessibility requirement?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I review the specification for loading, empty, error, disabled, responsive, and interaction states before coding. I raise focused questions with the designer or business analyst and record the agreed behavior. In implementation, I map the design to existing components and tokens so the result remains consistent and maintainable instead of becoming a one-off page.

questions.title: Working across delivery environments
questions.prompt: A frontend feature works in development but fails in the test or acceptance environment. How would you investigate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare environments, collect evidence, prevent recurrence.

expectedPoints.label: Compares environments systematically
expectedPoints.description: Reproduces the failure and compares configuration, build, API, permissions, and data.

expectedPoints.label: Uses diagnostic evidence
expectedPoints.description: Checks browser logs, network traces, application monitoring, and deployment records.

expectedPoints.label: Fixes and prevents recurrence
expectedPoints.description: Adds validation, tests, or configuration checks after finding the root cause.

followUps.prompt: What environment differences most often affect a frontend integration?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would reproduce the exact flow and compare the deployed build, runtime configuration, API endpoints, permissions, and representative data between environments. I would use browser console and network evidence together with application logs and deployment history. After identifying the root cause, I would add a regression test or automated configuration validation so the same mismatch is detected earlier.

questions.title: Technical documentation and handover
questions.prompt: What documentation would you provide when handing over a custom frontend or SPFx solution to another team?
questions.answerStructureHint: Architecture, operational runbook, knowledge transfer.

expectedPoints.label: Documents architecture and boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Explains components, data flow, external dependencies, and key decisions.

expectedPoints.label: Documents build and operation
expectedPoints.description: Covers setup, configuration, deployment, monitoring, and troubleshooting.

expectedPoints.label: Supports knowledge transfer
expectedPoints.description: Includes ownership, known limitations, examples, and a walkthrough.

followUps.prompt: How would you verify that the receiving team can actually support the solution?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would provide an architecture overview with component boundaries, data flow, dependencies, and important trade-offs. The operational section would cover local setup, configuration, building, deployment, monitoring, and common troubleshooting steps. I would also record ownership, known limitations, and open issues, then walk the receiving team through the solution and let them perform a real change or deployment.

questions.title: Explaining technical trade-offs
questions.prompt: How would you explain a frontend technical trade-off to a Tech Lead, Business Analyst, and non-technical stakeholder?
questions.answerStructureHint: Options, impact, recommendation.

expectedPoints.label: Presents realistic options
expectedPoints.description: Explains the available options rather than presenting one preference as the only answer.

expectedPoints.label: Connects technical choices to impact
expectedPoints.description: Relates choices to delivery time, risk, user experience, maintenance, and cost.

expectedPoints.label: Makes a clear recommendation
expectedPoints.description: States a recommendation, assumptions, and how the decision can be revisited.

followUps.prompt: Give an example of how you would explain code splitting differently to a developer and to a business stakeholder.

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would present two or three realistic options in language appropriate to the audience. I would connect each option to delivery time, user impact, operational risk, maintenance, and cost rather than focusing only on technical detail. I would then recommend one option, state the assumptions behind it, and record what evidence or future change would justify revisiting the decision.
